metadata.career_relevance: Every Indian fintech uses OAuth2. Razorpay's partner API uses Client Credentials flow — when HDFC Bank's system calls Razorpay programmatically, it authenticates with a client_id + client_secret and gets an access token scoped to only what HDFC is allowed to do. After this topic, you can implement both user-facing OAuth2 login and machine-to-machine OAuth2 for service APIs.

hook.real_world_problem:
Zerodha wants to integrate with a third-party tax filing service (Quicko). Users should be able to authorize Quicko to read their trading history WITHOUT sharing their Zerodha password.

```
Without OAuth2 (dangerous):
  User gives Quicko their Zerodha username/password
  Quicko stores the password → security breach risk
  Zerodha can't revoke access without password change

With OAuth2 Authorization Code flow:
  User → Zerodha login page → 'Authorize Quicko to read trading history'
  → Zerodha issues access_token (scoped: read:trades only)
  → Quicko uses token to read trades (never sees password)
  → User can revoke Quicko's access anytime in Zerodha settings
```
hook.what_you_will_build: OAuth2 resource server (validates tokens from an auth server) and OAuth2 client configuration for service-to-service Client Credentials flow.

concept_explanation.flows:
**OAuth2 Flows:**

```
AUTHORIZATION CODE (user-facing apps):
  1. User clicks 'Login with Google'
  2. App → Auth Server: GET /authorize?client_id=...&scope=email&redirect_uri=...
  3. User logs in + consents
  4. Auth Server → App: redirect_uri?code=AUTH_CODE
  5. App → Auth Server: POST /token (code + client_secret)
  6. Auth Server → App: { access_token, refresh_token }
  7. App uses access_token to call APIs

CLIENT CREDENTIALS (machine-to-machine):
  1. Service A → Auth Server: POST /token (client_id + client_secret + grant_type=client_credentials)
  2. Auth Server → Service A: { access_token }
  3. Service A calls Service B with access_token
  (No user involved — service authenticates as itself)
```
concept_explanation.spring_config:
**Spring OAuth2 Resource Server + Client:**

```yaml
# application.yml — Resource Server (validates tokens):
spring:
  security:
    oauth2:
      resourceserver:
        jwt:
          jwk-set-uri: https://auth.razorpay.com/.well-known/jwks.json
          issuer-uri: https://auth.razorpay.com

# application.yml — OAuth2 Client (gets tokens for outbound calls):
  security:
    oauth2:
      client:
        registration:
          hdfc-api:
            client-id: razorpay-service
            client-secret: ${HDFC_CLIENT_SECRET}
            authorization-grant-type: client_credentials
            scope: payments:read, settlements:write
        provider:
          hdfc-api:
            token-uri: https://api.hdfcbank.com/oauth/token
```

```java
// WebClient with OAuth2 token injection:
@Bean
WebClient hdfcClient(OAuth2AuthorizedClientManager mgr) {
    var oauth2 = new ServletOAuth2AuthorizedClientExchangeFilterFunction(mgr);
    oauth2.setDefaultClientRegistrationId("hdfc-api");
    return WebClient.builder()
        .baseUrl("https://api.hdfcbank.com")
        .apply(oauth2.oauth2Configuration())
        .build();
}
// WebClient automatically obtains + caches + refreshes the token!
```

visual_aid.description: Two flow diagrams side by side. Left: Authorization Code — User browser → Zerodha Auth Server (login+consent) → redirect with code → Quicko backend exchanges code for token → calls Zerodha API. Right: Client Credentials — HDFC Bank service → Razorpay Auth Server (client_id+secret) → access_token → calls Razorpay payment API. Both show scopes as labels on the token arrows.
visual_aid.alt_text: OAuth2 Authorization Code flow (user) vs Client Credentials flow (service-to-service)

worked_example.scenario: Razorpay payment service as OAuth2 resource server + WebClient using Client Credentials to call HDFC Bank API.
worked_example.problem_statement:
1. SecurityFilterChain: JWT resource server with scope-based access
2. WebClient: Client Credentials for HDFC API calls
3. @SpringBootTest: verify scope check works

step_by_step_solution.details: Roles (MERCHANT, ADMIN): who the user IS. Scopes (payments:read, settlements:write): what the application is ALLOWED to do on behalf of the user. @PreAuthorize: `hasRole('MERCHANT')` checks roles; `hasAuthority('SCOPE_payments:read')` checks scopes. Spring Security maps OAuth2 scopes to SCOPE_xxx GrantedAuthority automatically.

things_to_notice: SCOPE_ prefix: Spring Security automatically prefixes OAuth2 scopes with SCOPE_ when creating GrantedAuthority. So scope 'payments:read' becomes authority 'SCOPE_payments:read'. Use hasAuthority('SCOPE_payments:read'), not hasRole('payments:read').

Client Credentials caching: Spring's OAuth2AuthorizedClientManager automatically caches the access token and refreshes it before expiry. You don't manage token lifecycle manually — WebClient handles it transparently.

PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange): for Authorization Code flow in SPAs/mobile apps, PKCE prevents authorization code interception attacks. Spring Security 6+ enables it by default for public clients.

Token introspection vs JWT: JWT = self-contained (validated locally with public key). Opaque token = reference token, validated by calling auth server's /introspect endpoint. JWT is faster (no network call); opaque allows real-time revocation.

industry_examples.use_case: HDFC Bank, ICICI, Axis all integrate with Razorpay programmatically using Client Credentials flow. Each bank has a client_id scoped to: payments:initiate, refunds:read, settlements:read. A token from HDFC cannot initiate refunds at ICICI — scopes are bank-specific.

applications.interview_relevance: 'Explain the difference between OAuth2 Authorization Code and Client Credentials flows.' → Auth Code: user-facing, delegates user permission to a third-party app (Quicko reading Zerodha trades). Client Credentials: machine-to-machine, no user, service authenticates as itself (HDFC calling Razorpay). Auth Code has redirect URI and authorization step; Client Credentials is a single POST /token call.
applications.real_world_workflow: OAuth2 setup: (1) register client with auth server (get client_id + secret), (2) configure Spring Security resource server (validate incoming tokens), (3) configure WebClient with OAuth2 for outbound calls (auto token management), (4) use scopes for fine-grained access control on each endpoint.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Using hasRole('payments:read') instead of hasAuthority('SCOPE_payments:read'). OAuth2 scopes are NOT roles. They're different grant mechanisms.
common_gaps.remediation: Scopes → GrantedAuthority with SCOPE_ prefix (e.g., SCOPE_payments:read). Roles → GrantedAuthority with ROLE_ prefix (e.g., ROLE_MERCHANT). Use hasAuthority() for scopes, hasRole() for roles. Both can be in the same token.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: hasRole('payments:read') always returns 403 even with correct scope — missing the SCOPE_ prefix

key_takeaways: Auth Code flow: user-facing, consent-based, includes redirect
Client Credentials: machine-to-machine, no user, single token request
Scopes → SCOPE_xxx authority; Roles → ROLE_xxx authority
PKCE: required for SPAs/mobile apps (prevents code interception)
Opaque token: requires /introspect call; JWT: validated locally

self_check_questions.question: When should you use Authorization Code flow vs Client Credentials?
self_check_questions.answer: Authorization Code: when a user needs to delegate access to a third-party application. The user must actively consent. Example: user allows Quicko to read their Zerodha trades. The application gets a token that represents the user's delegated permission. Client Credentials: when a service calls another service with no user involved. Example: Razorpay's reconciliation service calling its own settlements service at 2am (no user is present). The service authenticates as itself using client_id + client_secret. Rule: if there's a human consenting → Auth Code. If it's automated service-to-service → Client Credentials.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: Secrets Management with HashiCorp Vault — storing database passwords, API keys, and certificates securely instead of in application.yml or environment variables.
